title: Cat Swollen Hock Joint: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's ankle swollen? Learn about hock joint swelling, urgency levels, and why photos are vital for veterinary triage and care.
content_html:
TL;DR: A swollen hock joint in cats is generally a medium-urgency issue requiring a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours, though immediate emergency care is necessary if your cat has a high fever, severe pain, or an open wound.

What causes my cat to have a swollen hock joint?

  • The hock joint is the equivalent of a human ankle, located on your cat's back leg above the paw.
  • Swelling in this area typically indicates inflammation, fluid buildup, or localized trauma.
  • Common causes range from soft tissue sprains and ligament tears to more serious issues like feline arthritis, bone fractures, or infected bite wounds that have developed into abscesses.

Is a swollen hock joint considered a veterinary emergency?

  • A swollen hock joint is classified as a Medium Urgency situation.
  • If your cat is still eating and behaving normally despite a limp, you should generally aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if the swelling is accompanied by a high fever, a visible open fracture, or if your cat is crying out in pain and refusing to move.

How does sharing a photo or video help with the veterinary triage process?

  • Sharing a high-quality photo or a short video of your cat walking helps the veterinary team see the exact location of the swelling.
  • Visuals allow professionals to check for skin discoloration or puncture wounds and assess the severity of the lameness.
  • This information helps the clinic determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and what diagnostic tools, such as X-rays, might be required upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

While the provided text excerpts relate to hock issues in horses, they do not specifically address "Cat Swollen Hock Joint: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide". Therefore, I am unable to provide a relevant paragraph.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human pain medications like aspirin, ibuprofen, or Tylenol, as these are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal.

faqs.question: How can I make my cat comfortable until the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Restrict your cat's movement by keeping them in a small room or a large carrier to prevent jumping. Ensure their food, water, and litter box are all within easy reach.

faqs.question: Will the swelling go down on its own?
faqs.answer: While minor strains may improve with rest, swelling often masks underlying issues like infections or fractures that require medical intervention to heal properly.
